title: Strain and Twist Engineering of Interfacial Thermal Transport in Homo- and Hetero-Interfaces of Graphene and Hexagonal Boron Nitride
authors: Wenwu Jiang, Huasong Qin, Yilun Liu, Wengen Ouyang, Oded Hod, Michael Urbakh
abstract: A dramatic difference between the vertical thermal conductance response of homogeneous and heterogeneous graphene/h-BN interfaces to external mechanical perturbations, is predicted. Homogeneous graphene and h-BN interfaces exhibit strong conductance reduction for both in-plane strain and interfacial twist. Conversely, the vertical thermal conductance of the heterogeneous graphene/h-BN junction is insensitive to twist deformations but shows significant increase or decrease under compressive or tensile strains, respectively. Our atomistic simulations predictions are rationalized by Fermi's golden rule and density of phonon modes analyses, indicating that vertical phonons and local stacking configurations have a central role in the interlayer heat transport behavior. A simple phenomenological model, based on local interlayer distance and stacking, captures well the dependence of vertical heat conductance on strain and twist deformations.
summary: This paper explores how mechanical strain and twisting can be used to engineer heat transport in 2D material interfaces. It reveals a fundamental difference between similar material stacks (graphene/graphene) and different material stacks (graphene/h-BN), where the latter is uniquely sensitive to strain but not twist. The researchers provide a simple model based on local atomic distances and stacking to predict these thermal properties. This work is crucial for designing efficient heat dissipation strategies in future nanoscale electronic and spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how mechanical perturbations, specifically in-plane strain and interfacial twist, affect the vertical thermal conductance of both homogeneous (graphene/graphene, h-BN/h-BN) and heterogeneous (graphene/h-BN) van der Waals interfaces.
main_result: Homogeneous interfaces show reduced thermal conductance under both strain and twist, whereas heterogeneous graphene/h-BN interfaces are insensitive to twist but show significant conductance changes under strain (increase under compression, decrease under tension).
method: Atomistic simulations using Non-Equilibrium Molecular Dynamics (NEMD) with the LAMMPS package, supplemented by Fermi's golden rule and density of phonon modes analysis.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 16-layer van der Waals stacks of graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), utilizing anisotropic interlayer potentials and REBO/Tersoff intralayer potentials.
key_observables: Vertical thermal conductivity (kappa_perp), interfacial thermal conductance (ITC), frequency-resolved thermal conductance G(omega), and local registry index (LRI).
important_parameters: In-plane biaxial lateral strain (epsilon), interfacial twist angle (theta), and local interlayer distance (d_i).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the atomistic potentials (REBO and Tersoff) and uses a phenomenological model based on local geometric characteristics to capture the transport behavior.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the simulation setup, vertical thermal conductivity as a function of strain, and normalized ITC comparisons. Figure 2 displays frequency-resolved thermal conductance under various strains. Figure 3 shows the dependence of vertical thermal conductivity on specific parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal management in vdW heterostructures, describes the simulation methodology and physical models, presents results for homogeneous and heterogeneous interfaces, provides a physical mechanism via phonon analysis, and concludes with a validated phenomenological model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
